content:
# Exodus Chapter 34 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 104-106; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 77-78 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_034_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_034_page_002.jpg`, `EXO_034_page_003.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_034_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_034_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## New Tablets and the Theophany Prepared (vv. 1-4)

**1** And YHWH said to Moses, "Carve for yourself two tablets of stone like the first ones, and I will write upon the tablets the words which were upon the first tablets which you shattered."

**2** "And be prepared for the morning, and go up in the morning to Mount Sinai, and station yourself for me there upon the top of the mountain."

**3** "And no man shall go up with you, and also no man shall be seen in all the mountain; also the flock and the cattle shall not graze before that mountain."

**4** And he carved two tablets of stone like the first ones; and Moses rose early in the morning and went up to Mount Sinai as YHWH had commanded him, and he took in his hand two tablets of stone.

---

## The Proclamation of the Name (vv. 5-9)

**5** And YHWH descended in the cloud and stationed himself with him there, and he proclaimed the name of YHWH.

**6** And YHWH passed before his face and proclaimed: "YHWH, YHWH, a God compassionate and gracious, slow of anger and abundant of loyal-love and faithfulness --

**7** keeping loyal-love for thousands, bearing iniquity and transgression and sin; and acquitting, he will certainly not acquit -- visiting the iniquity of fathers upon sons and upon sons of sons, upon the third generation and upon the fourth generation."

**8** And Moses hastened and bowed his head toward the ground and bowed down.

**9** And he said, "If indeed I have found favor in your eyes, O Lord, let the Lord, I pray, go in our midst; for it is a stiff-necked people; and you shall pardon our iniquity and our sin, and you shall take us as your inheritance."

---

## The Covenant Stipulations (vv. 10-26)

**10** And he said, "Behold, I am cutting a covenant. Before all your people I will do wonders which have not been created in all the earth and in all the nations; and all the people in whose midst you are shall see the work of YHWH, for it is an awesome thing -- that which I am doing with you."

**11** "Keep for yourself that which I am commanding you today. Behold, I am driving out before you the Amorite, and the Canaanite, and the Hittite, and the Perizzite, and the Hivite, and the Jebusite."

**12** "Guard yourself lest you cut a covenant with the inhabitant of the land upon which you are entering, lest it become a snare in your midst."

**13** "Rather, their altars you shall tear down, and their pillars you shall shatter, and their Asherah-poles you shall cut down."

**14** "For you shall not bow down to another god, for YHWH -- Jealous is his name -- he is a jealous God."

**15** "Lest you cut a covenant with the inhabitant of the land, and they prostitute themselves after their gods, and they sacrifice to their gods, and one calls you and you eat from his sacrifice --"

**16** "-- and you take from his daughters for your sons, and his daughters prostitute themselves after their gods, and they cause your sons to prostitute themselves after their gods."

**17** "Molten gods you shall not make for yourself."

**18** "The Festival of Unleavened-bread you shall keep. Seven days you shall eat unleavened-bread as I commanded you, at the appointed time of the month of Aviv, for in the month of Aviv you went out from Egypt."

**19** "Every opener of a womb is mine, and all your livestock that is remembered as male -- the opener of ox and sheep."

**20** "And the opener of a donkey you shall redeem with a lamb; and if you do not redeem it, then you shall break its neck. Every firstborn of your sons you shall redeem; and they shall not appear before my face empty-handed."

**21** "Six days you shall work, and on the seventh day you shall rest; in plowing time and in harvest you shall rest."

**22** "And the Festival of Weeks you shall observe -- the firstfruits of the wheat harvest -- and the Festival of Ingathering at the turn of the year."

**23** "Three times in the year every male of yours shall appear before the face of the Lord, YHWH, God of Israel."

**24** "For I will dispossess nations before you and I will enlarge your border; and no man shall covet your land when you go up to appear before the face of YHWH your God three times in the year."

**25** "You shall not slaughter upon leaven the blood of my sacrifice; and the sacrifice of the Festival of Passover shall not remain overnight until morning."

**26** "The first of the firstfruits of your ground you shall bring to the house of YHWH your God. You shall not boil a kid in the milk of its mother."

---

## The Writing of the Covenant and Moses' Shining Face (vv. 27-35)

**27** And YHWH said to Moses, "Write for yourself these words, for upon the mouth of these words I have cut a covenant with you and with Israel."

**28** And he was there with YHWH forty days and forty nights; bread he did not eat and water he did not drink. And he wrote upon the tablets the words of the covenant -- the Ten Words.

**29** And it was, when Moses descended from Mount Sinai -- and the two tablets of the testimony were in the hand of Moses when he descended from the mountain -- and Moses did not know that the skin of his face shone when he spoke with him.

**30** And Aaron and all the Sons of Israel saw Moses, and behold, the skin of his face shone, and they were afraid to come near to him.

**31** And Moses called to them, and Aaron and all the leaders in the congregation returned to him, and Moses spoke to them.

**32** And afterward all the Sons of Israel drew near, and he commanded them all that YHWH had spoken with him on Mount Sinai.

**33** And Moses finished speaking with them, and he put upon his face a veil.

**34** And when Moses entered before the face of YHWH to speak with him, he would remove the veil until he came out; and he would come out and speak to the Sons of Israel that which he was commanded.

**35** And the Sons of Israel saw the face of Moses, that the skin of the face of Moses shone; and Moses would put back the veil upon his face until he entered to speak with him.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Observations

The Leningrad Codex (B19A) presents Exodus 34 across folios 104-106 (three page images), continuing directly from YHWH's promise to pass his goodness before Moses at the close of chapter 33. This chapter is the theological summit of the golden calf narrative (chs. 32-34) and arguably of the entire Torah's portrait of YHWH's character. A parashah setumah mark is visible before v. 1 on page 104. The divine proclamation (vv. 6-7) occupies a prominent position in the text block; a masorah parva note appears beside v. 6 at the divine attributes passage. The Masoretic accentuation on v. 6 places the atnach (major disjunctive accent) after ve-emet, dividing the proclamation into two balanced halves: the positive attributes before the atnach, and the acts of forgiveness and justice after. The festival laws (vv. 18-26) occupy the central and left columns of page 105. A petuhah (open paragraph break) appears before v. 27, separating the stipulations from the covenant writing. Another division appears before v. 29, introducing the veil narrative as a distinct unit. The shining-face narrative (vv. 29-35) concludes on page 106. Vowel pointing and cantillation marks are intact throughout.

The Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) presents the LXX text of Exodus 34 across pages 77-78 in the standard three-column uncial scriptio continua format. The chapter heading KZ is visible at the top of page 77. The Vaticanus text follows the MT verse structure closely. Notable divergences: (1) In v. 6, Vaticanus reads kurios kurios ho theos for the divine self-proclamation, adding ho theos where the Hebrew has only the doubled name followed by El. (2) In v. 7, the LXX omits "and sin" (ve-khatta'ah), listing only two categories of forgiveness (iniquity and transgression) against the MT's three. (3) In v. 21, the LXX substitutes "sowing" (spora) for "plowing" (kharish). (4) In v. 26, the LXX renders gedi ("kid") as arna ("lamb"). (5) In v. 28, Vaticanus does not clarify the subject of "he wrote," leaving the same ambiguity as the MT. (6) In v. 29, Vaticanus reads dedoxastai ("was glorified") for the Hebrew qaran ("shone/sent forth rays"), interpreting the radiance theologically as glory rather than physical luminescence, and avoiding the qeren ("horn/ray") ambiguity entirely. In all cases, SOPHIA follows the MT as primary text.

### Theological Architecture of the Chapter

Exodus 34 is the resolution of the golden calf crisis (chs. 32-34) and the theological summit of the Sinai narrative. Where chapter 32 presented catastrophe (the shattered covenant) and chapter 33 presented negotiation (the plea for presence), chapter 34 presents restoration: new tablets, the divine name proclaimed, covenant renewed, and the aftereffect of divine encounter inscribed on Moses' very face.

The chapter moves through four movements: (1) the preparation of new tablets and the command to ascend alone (vv. 1-4), mirroring Exodus 19 and signaling genuine covenant restoration; (2) the proclamation of the divine name and attributes (vv. 5-9), the theological center of the entire Torah; (3) the covenant stipulations (vv. 10-26), reestablishing the terms of relationship; and (4) the writing of the covenant and Moses' shining face (vv. 27-35), the physical evidence that the broken relationship has been restored and that divine presence has transformative power.

The Thirteen Attributes of vv. 6-7 became the most frequently cited divine self-description in the Hebrew Bible, echoed in Numbers 14:18, Nehemiah 9:17, Psalms 86:15, 103:8, 145:8, Joel 2:13, Jonah 4:2, and Nahum 1:3 -- always with selective adaptation. The formula's deliberate asymmetry -- loyal-love extending to thousands of generations, consequences reaching only to the third and fourth -- establishes mercy as the dominant note while justice remains real. The infinitive absolute construction in v. 7 (ve-naqqeh lo yenaqqeh, "and acquitting, he will certainly not acquit") is the hinge between the two: YHWH forgives (nose avon), yet guilt has consequences that are not simply erased.

The shining face of Moses (vv. 29-35) forms the literary counterpart to the golden calf of chapter 32. Where the calf was a false, manufactured representation of the divine, Moses' radiant face is the authentic, unmanufactured effect of genuine encounter with YHWH. The veil functions to shield the people from the visible evidence of divine encounter -- a continuation of the "you cannot see my face and live" motif from 33:20. Paul's extended midrash on this passage in 2 Corinthians 3:7-18 builds on the LXX's doxa ("glory") framework, adding a purpose not stated in the Hebrew (that the glory was fading). The MT itself holds no such claim -- it simply describes the recurring practice.

### Key Consensus Decisions

1. **"Loyal-love" for khesed (vv. 6-7):** EMET renders khesed as "loyal-love," LOGOS as "steadfast love," and PNEUMA as "loyal love." SOPHIA adopts "loyal-love" (hyphenated, following EMET), because it preserves both the relational fidelity (loyalty) and the affective warmth (love) of this theologically central term. "Steadfast love" (LOGOS, following ESV/RSV tradition) is accurate but slightly more abstract. The hyphen signals that this is a single Hebrew concept, not two separate qualities. The LXX's eleos ("mercy") loses the covenantal dimension entirely.

2. **"Faithfulness" for emet (v. 6):** EMET renders emet as "truth," LOGOS as "faithfulness," PNEUMA as "faithfulness." SOPHIA adopts "faithfulness" because in the hendiadys khesed ve-emet, the paired terms describe covenant character -- loyal-love and reliability. While emet elsewhere means "truth," in this construction its primary force is relational dependability. "Truth" (EMET) is lexically defensible but could mislead an English reader into thinking of propositional truth rather than personal reliability.

3. **"Abundant of" vs. "overflowing with" (v. 6):** EMET and LOGOS render rav-khesed as "abundant of loyal-love" / "great of steadfast love." PNEUMA renders "overflowing with loyal love." SOPHIA adopts "abundant of loyal-love" to preserve the Hebrew construct form (rav + noun) and to maintain formal consistency with "slow of anger" (erekh appayim). PNEUMA's "overflowing" is vivid but introduces a liquid metaphor absent from the Hebrew.

4. **Infinitive absolute: "acquitting, he will certainly not acquit" (v. 7):** All three translators preserve the doubling in different forms. EMET: "he will certainly not acquit." LOGOS: "acquitting he will surely not acquit." PNEUMA: "who will by no means leave the guilty unpunished." SOPHIA adopts a rendering close to LOGOS that preserves the infinitive absolute doubling per binding conventions: "acquitting, he will certainly not acquit." PNEUMA's periphrastic rendering loses the Hebrew grammatical structure entirely.

5. **"Bearing" for nose (v. 7):** EMET renders "bearing," LOGOS renders "forgiving," PNEUMA renders "who forgives." SOPHIA adopts "bearing" (EMET) because the Qal participle of nasa literally means "lifting/carrying away" -- the image is of YHWH taking up and removing the burden of sin. "Forgiving" (LOGOS) is the theological result but loses the physical image. The same verb is used for bearing a load (Gen 13:6, Isa 1:14), and preserving the metaphor allows the English reader to feel the weight of what YHWH does with Israel's sin.

6. **"Take us as your inheritance" (v. 9):** EMET and LOGOS render u-nekhalitanu as "take us as an inheritance" / "take us as an inheritance." PNEUMA renders "claim us as your own." SOPHIA adopts "take us as your inheritance" to preserve the nachal (inheritance/possession) concept -- Moses asks YHWH to make Israel his own hereditary estate. PNEUMA's "claim us as your own" captures the theology but loses the inheritance vocabulary. The LXX's kleronomeison hemas ("inherit us") confirms the inheritance sense.

7. **"Shone" for qaran (vv. 29-30, 35):** All three translators render qaran as "shone" or "radiating light" / "radiant." SOPHIA adopts "shone" as the most accurate English equivalent of the Hebrew denominative verb from qeren ("horn/ray"). The verb describes emitting beams of light. Jerome's Vulgate rendering cornuta ("horned") produced Michelangelo's iconic but mistaken horned Moses. The LXX's dedoxastai ("was glorified") is interpretive but theologically sound. "Shone" is neutral, accurate, and avoids both the horn-ambiguity and the LXX's theological gloss.

8. **Subject ambiguity in v. 28:** The subject of "he wrote upon the tablets" is grammatically ambiguous. EMET and LOGOS preserve the ambiguity with "he wrote." PNEUMA resolves it, making YHWH the subject. SOPHIA preserves the MT's ambiguity with "he wrote," following the principle that translation should not resolve what the source text leaves open. The most coherent reading -- that YHWH wrote the Ten Words on the tablets (cf. v. 1) while Moses wrote the covenant stipulations (v. 27) -- is a matter of interpretation, properly addressed in commentary rather than translation.

9. **"Veil" for masveh (vv. 33-35):** All three translators agree on "veil." This hapax legomenon (in usage context) appears only in Exodus 34:33-35. Its etymology is uncertain, possibly from the root savah ("to cover"). The LXX's kalumma ("covering/veil") is the term Paul uses in 2 Corinthians 3:13-16.

10. **Iterative verbal forms (vv. 34-35):** EMET and LOGOS render the imperfect verbs as habitual ("he would remove," "he would come out," "he would put back"). PNEUMA uses similar phrasing ("whenever Moses went in... he would remove"). SOPHIA follows the habitual "would" phrasing to convey the repeated, ongoing nature of the practice, distinguishing it from the single-occurrence narrative of vv. 29-33.
